([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Schloss Nymphenburg*, Palace of the [Nymphs](/wiki/Nymph "Nymph")) is a [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") [palace](/wiki/Palace "Palace") situated in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich")'s western district [Neuhausen-Nymphenburg](/wiki/Neuhausen-Nymphenburg "Neuhausen-Nymphenburg"), in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), southern [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). The Nymphenburg served as the main summer residence for the [former rulers of Bavaria](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Bavaria "List of rulers of Bavaria") of the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach"). Combined with the adjacent [Nymphenburg Palace Park](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace_Park "Nymphenburg Palace Park") it constitutes one of the premier royal palaces of Europe. Its frontal width of 632 m (2,073 ft) (north–south axis) even surpasses [Versailles](/wiki/Versailles "Versailles").

The **Nymphenburg Palace** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Schloss Nymphenburg*, Palace of the [Nymphs](/wiki/Nymph "Nymph")) is a [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") [palace](/wiki/Palace "Palace") situated in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich")'s western district [Neuhausen-Nymphenburg](/wiki/Neuhausen-Nymphenburg "Neuhausen-Nymphenburg"), in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), southern [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). The Nymphenburg served as the main summer residence for the [former rulers of Bavaria](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Bavaria "List of rulers of Bavaria") of the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach"). Combined with the adjacent [Nymphenburg Palace Park](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Palace_Park "Nymphenburg Palace Park") it constitutes one of the premier royal palaces of Europe. Its frontal width of 632 m (2,073 ft) (north–south axis) even surpasses [Versailles](/wiki/Versailles "Versailles").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Building history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Building history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Canaletto_%28I%29_020.jpg/330px-Canaletto_%28I%29_020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Canaletto_(I)_020.jpg)

Nymphenburg Palace, around 1760, as painted by [Bernardo Bellotto](/wiki/Bernardo_Bellotto "Bernardo Bellotto")

The palace was commissioned by the [electoral](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") couple [Ferdinand Maria](/wiki/Ferdinand_Maria,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Ferdinand Maria, Elector of Bavaria") and [Henriette Adelaide of Savoy](/wiki/Henriette_Adelaide_of_Savoy "Henriette Adelaide of Savoy") to the designs of the Italian architect [Agostino Barelli](/wiki/Agostino_Barelli "Agostino Barelli") in 1664 after the birth of their son [Maximilian II Emanuel](/wiki/Maximilian_II_Emanuel,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian II Emanuel, Elector of Bavaria").[[1]](#cite_note-1) During its construction Barelli was again replaced (1674) by [Enrico Zuccalli](/wiki/Enrico_Zuccalli "Enrico Zuccalli").[[2]](#cite_note-Grove-2) The concept for the mythological decorative programme was supplied by the scholar [Emanuele Tesauro](/wiki/Emanuele_Tesauro "Emanuele Tesauro") of Turin; the ceiling paintings were by [Antonio Triva](/wiki/Antonio_Triva "Antonio Triva") and [Antonio Zanchi](/wiki/Antonio_Zanchi "Antonio Zanchi").[[2]](#cite_note-Grove-2) The central pavilion was completed in 1675. As a building material, it utilised limestone from [Kelheim](/wiki/Kelheim "Kelheim"). The palace was gradually expanded and transformed over the years.[[3]](#cite_note-sch-3) It then quickly replaced the nearby [Blutenburg Castle](/wiki/Blutenburg_Castle "Blutenburg Castle") as major hunting lodge of the court and competed to [Schleissheim Palace](/wiki/Schleissheim_Palace "Schleissheim Palace").

Starting in 1701, Maximilian Emanuel, the heir to Bavaria, a sovereign [electorate](/wiki/Electorate_of_Bavaria "Electorate of Bavaria") of the Holy Roman Empire, undertook a systematic extension of the palace. Two pavilions were added each in the south and north of Barelli's palace by [Enrico Zucalli](/wiki/Enrico_Zucalli "Enrico Zucalli") and [Giovanni Antonio Viscardi](/wiki/Giovanni_Antonio_Viscardi "Giovanni Antonio Viscardi") and were connected with the centre pavilion by two gallery wings. In 1716, [Joseph Effner](/wiki/Joseph_Effner "Joseph Effner") redesigned the facade of the centre pavilion in [French Baroque](/wiki/French_Baroque "French Baroque") style with [pilasters](/wiki/Pilasters "Pilasters"). Later, the south section of the palace was further extended to build the court stables (1719). For the sake of balance, the orangery building was added to the north which was only completed in 1758. Finally, Nymphenburg Palace was completed with a grand circle (the *Schlossrondell*) of Baroque mansions (the so-called Kavaliershäuschen – cavalier's lodges), erected under Maximilian Emanuel's son Holy Roman Emperor [Charles VII Albert](/wiki/Charles_VII,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VII, Holy Roman Emperor") in the palace's driveway. His son Prince-Elector [Maximilian III](/wiki/Maximilian_III_Joseph,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian III Joseph, Elector of Bavaria") founded the [Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Porcelain_Manufactory "Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory") in 1747, to this day housed in one of these cavalier's lodges. Its fashionable Rococo products by porcelain sculptors [Franz Anton Bustelli](/wiki/Franz_Anton_Bustelli "Franz Anton Bustelli") and [Dominik Auliczek](/wiki/Dominik_Auliczek "Dominik Auliczek") made the name *Nymphenburg* widely known.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/M-Nymphenburg-LudwigII-Geburtszimmer.JPG/250px-M-Nymphenburg-LudwigII-Geburtszimmer.JPG)](/wiki/File:M-Nymphenburg-LudwigII-Geburtszimmer.JPG)

Birthroom of King [Ludwig II of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria")

In 1795, [Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria") ordered the widening of the galleries on the park side. In 1826, under King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I of Bavaria"), his architect [Leo von Klenze](/wiki/Leo_von_Klenze "Leo von Klenze") removed the gables of the main pavilion with the Electoral coat of arms and created an [attic style](/wiki/Attic_style "Attic style") decoration directly under the roof instead.[[3]](#cite_note-sch-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/5/5f/Luitpoldfam.jpg/250px-Luitpoldfam.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luitpoldfam.jpg)

[Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria](/wiki/Luitpold,_Prince_Regent_of_Bavaria "Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria") with his son [Ludwig](/wiki/Ludwig_III_of_Bavaria "Ludwig III of Bavaria"), his grandson [Rupprecht](/wiki/Rupprecht,_Crown_Prince_of_Bavaria "Rupprecht, Crown Prince of Bavaria") and his great-grandson Luitpold in the park of Nymphenburg Palace, about 1910

### Residence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Residence")]

With the Treaty of Nymphenburg signed in July 1741, Charles Albert allied with France and Spain against Austria. Two of his children were born here: [Maria Antonia](/wiki/Maria_Antonia_Walpurgis_of_Bavaria "Maria Antonia Walpurgis of Bavaria") (future Electress of Saxony) in 1724 and [Maria Anna Josepha](/wiki/Duchess_Maria_Anna_Josepha_of_Bavaria "Duchess Maria Anna Josepha of Bavaria") (future Margravine of Baden-Baden) in 1734. Charles Albert lived during his time in Munich as [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") at Nymphenburg Palace and died there in 1745. In 1747, Elector [Max III. Joseph](/wiki/Maximilian_III_Joseph,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian III Joseph, Elector of Bavaria") founded the [Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Porcelain_Manufactory "Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory"). In 1792, Elector Charles Theodor opened the park for the public.

For a long time, the palace was the favourite summer residence of the rulers of Bavaria. King [Max I Joseph](/wiki/Maximilian_I_Joseph_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I Joseph of Bavaria") died there in 1825[[5]](#cite_note-5) and his great-grandson King [Ludwig II](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria") was born there in 1845.[[6]](#cite_note-6) In 1863, the only meeting between Ludwig and [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") was held in Nymphenburg, although they remained connected in a lifelong friendship.

Today, Nymphenburg is open to the public but also continues to be a home and [chancery](/wiki/Chancery_(diplomacy) "Chancery (diplomacy)") for the head of the [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach"), currently [Franz, Duke of Bavaria](/wiki/Franz,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Franz, Duke of Bavaria").[[7]](#cite_note-Sckell1840-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Palace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Palace")]

The palace, together with its park, is now one of the most famous sights in Munich. The baroque facades comprise an overall width of about 700 metres. Some rooms still show their original [baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") decoration while others were later redesigned in [rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") or [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") style.

### Central pavilion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Central pavilion")]

The *Steinerner Saal* (Stone Hall) in the *central pavilion*, with ceiling frescoes by [Johann Baptist Zimmermann](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Zimmermann "Johann Baptist Zimmermann") and F. Zimmermann and decorations by [François de Cuvilliés](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Cuvilli%C3%A9s "François de Cuvilliés"), is an impressive sight. Acting as a grand hall, it occupies over three floors of the central pavilion of the palace. The central ceiling fresco is [Helios](/wiki/Helios "Helios") in his chariot, accompanied by other gods.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Nymphenburg_Munich_Steinerne_Saal.jpg/250px-Nymphenburg_Munich_Steinerne_Saal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nymphenburg_Munich_Steinerne_Saal.jpg)

Steinerner Saal (Marble Hall)

North of the Stone Hall, there is the wood-panelled antechamber, the audience chamber decorated with Brussels tapestries and the former bedroom with the so-called *Little Beauty Gallery* with the ladies of [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles"); all rooms were remodelled under Maximilian II Emanuel in the [style of the Régence](/wiki/R%C3%A9gence "Régence") but retain their original Baroque ceilings. Here are on display portraits of the elector and his wife [Theresa Kunegunda Sobieska](/wiki/Theresa_Kunegunda_Sobieska "Theresa Kunegunda Sobieska"). The bedroom closes the park side, next to it is the *Drechsel Cabinet* (turnery cabinet) of [Maximilian III Joseph](/wiki/Maximilian_III,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian III, Elector of Bavaria"), designed by François de Cuvilliés. Three rooms further to the north were created under [Charles Theodore](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria") with the widening of the gallery wing. In the first room there are now more portraits of ladies from the *Great Gallery of Beauties* of Max Emanuel, the second one is decorated with a pile rug with the coats of arms of Bavaria and the Palatinate (known as "coat of arms room"), while the third room contains portraits of Charles Theodore and both his consorts [Elisabeth Auguste](/wiki/Countess_Palatine_Elisabeth_Auguste_of_Sulzbach "Countess Palatine Elisabeth Auguste of Sulzbach") and [Maria Leopoldine](/wiki/Archduchess_Maria_Leopoldine_of_Austria-Este "Archduchess Maria Leopoldine of Austria-Este").[[3]](#cite_note-sch-3)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Chinesisches_Kabinett%2C_Nymphenburg.jpg/250px-Chinesisches_Kabinett%2C_Nymphenburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinesisches_Kabinett,_Nymphenburg.jpg)

The Chinese Cabinet with [chinoiserie](/wiki/Chinoiserie "Chinoiserie"), one of the rooms of Nymphenburg Palace

South of the Stone Hall are inversely to the northern rooms of the main building, the hall with the portrait of [Charles Albert](/wiki/Charles_VII,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VII, Holy Roman Emperor"), the audience room with the portrait of the founding couple Ferdinand Maria and his consort Henriette Adelaide and the Former Bedroom with portraits of Max Emanuel and his consort Theresa Kunegunda. Here too, the original Baroque ceilings have survived. The walls of the so-called *lacquer cabinet* that adjoins the bedroom are almost completely covered with Chinese panels showing scenes from a Chinese novel. The stucco was done by [Franz Xaver Feuchtmayer the Younger](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_Feuchtmayer_the_Younger "Franz Xaver Feuchtmayer the Younger"). Behind the south gallery are the *Writing Cabinet* and *Antechamber* of Elector Charles Theodore, which were created with the widening of the gallery wings.

In both the **North and South Galleries** next to the *Central Pavilion* are [vedutes](/wiki/Veduta "Veduta") of Bavarian castles. These galleries connect the central pavilion with the southern and northern pavilions.

### Southern pavilions and wings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Southern pavilions and wings")]

The **Inner southern Pavilion** housed the apartments of the Electress during the period of its origin. The former small dining room of the Inner Southern Pavilion today houses the famous attraction *[Gallery of Beauties](/wiki/Gallery_of_Beauties "Gallery of Beauties")* of King [Ludwig I of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_I,_King_of_Bavaria "Ludwig I, King of Bavaria"). On behalf of the king the court painter [Joseph Karl Stieler](/wiki/Joseph_Karl_Stieler "Joseph Karl Stieler") portrayed 36 beautiful women from all social classes of Munich, the best known of these are the shoemaker's daughter Helene Sedlmayr and Ludwig's infamous mistress [Lola Montez](/wiki/Lola_Montez "Lola Montez"). In the nearby *Queen's bedroom* one can see where King [Ludwig II of Bavaria](/wiki/Ludwig_II_of_Bavaria "Ludwig II of Bavaria") was born on 25 August 1845. Its mahogany furniture was made in 1815 in Munich, unlike the mahogany furniture for Queen [Caroline's](/wiki/Caroline_of_Baden "Caroline of Baden") *audience room* which was made in Paris, as was the furniture in the *Queen's Study*.

The **Outer Southern Pavilion** is generally inaccessible. It served as a kitchen building at Max Emanuel's time and was then reconstructed like the inner pavilion in neo-classical times. Further south, the third pavilion was built as a *comedihaus* and then served from 1750 as a new kitchen house. The *southern corridor* built in 1747 connects this building with the stables in the south wing.

In the former royal stables in the **South Wing** is the [Marstallmuseum](/wiki/Marstallmuseum "Marstallmuseum") (carriage museum), with one of the greatest coach collections in Europe. They also played a part in historical events - the Paris Coronation Coach for example was used for the coronation of Emperor Charles VII in 1742. Among the main attractions of the museum are the magnificent carriages and sleighs of King Ludwig II.

The first floor of the former court stables houses a collection of *Nymphenburg porcelain* by the [Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Porcelain_Manufactory "Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory") which, also located in the palace complex, was founded by Maximilian III Joseph. Its handcrafted products are of legendary kind and quality, nowadays said to be comparable only to Augarten and Sèvres. Over 1,000 exhibits, beginning in 1747, are on display. The Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory itself is located in and behind one of the cavalier houses of the northern roundabouts. [Prince Luitpold](/wiki/Luitpold_Prinz_von_Bayern "Luitpold Prinz von Bayern"), the current owner of the manufactory, converted the cavalier house itself, which previously housed the manufactory's administration (production takes place in buildings at the rear), into a luxury holiday home in 2025, available for daily rental, under the name *Nymphenburg Palace Royal Residence*. It is managed by [Kempinski Hotels](/wiki/Kempinski_Hotels "Kempinski Hotels").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In the adjoining **Outer South Wing** of the castle is a restaurant with beer garden.

### Northern pavilions and wings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Northern pavilions and wings")]

The **Inner Northern Pavilion**, the later so-called *Crown Prince Building*, is generally inaccessible. Here was Max Emanuel's *appartement de parade* and its representative rooms are today used by the *Wittelsbach Compensation Fund*. The upper floors serve as living quarters for the respective head of the House of [Wittelsbach](/wiki/Wittelsbach "Wittelsbach"). The **Outer Northern Pavilion** houses the chapel, whose ceiling painting by Joseph Adam von Mölk deals with the life of St Mary Magdalene. It was already begun in 1702 by Antonio Viscardi from the design by Enrico Zuccalli. Further north is the third pavilion, the *Gardemeublebau* from 1723, an elongated building which served during the period of its origin for the [Cue sports](/wiki/Cue_sports "Cue sports") and the *Jeu de Passe*, a ball game inspired by [Pall-mall](/wiki/Pall-mall "Pall-mall"), which was invented by Max Emanuel himself and was played indoors and outdoors. Today the Duke of Bavaria's administration is located here. It is connected to the north wing by the *northern corridor* of 1739.

Since 1990, the *[Museum of Man and Nature](/wiki/Museum_of_Man_and_Nature "Museum of Man and Nature")* has been housed in the **North Wing**. The *Hubertus Hall* upstairs served for concerts. Today the Hubertus Hall, the Orangery Hall, and the Johannis Hall in the North Wing as well as the Iron House in the park can be booked for parties, concerts, conferences and other functions. From 1835 the Mary Ward Elementary School was in the adjoining **Outer North Wing** of the castle. Founded by [Mary Ward](/wiki/Mary_Ward_(nun) "Mary Ward (nun)"), it paved the way for a better education for girls. Mary Ward came after travelling from Rome to Munich in 1627 and was sponsored by Elector [Maximilian I](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I, Elector of Bavaria"). King Ludwig I finally invited the girls' school to the Nymphenburg Palace in 1835.[[7]](#cite_note-Sckell1840-7)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Park")]

### Garden structure

The 200-hectare (490-acre) park, once an [Italian garden](/wiki/Italian_garden "Italian garden") (1671), which was enlarged and rearranged in [French style](/wiki/French_formal_garden "French formal garden") by [Dominique Girard](/wiki/Dominique_Girard_(architect) "Dominique Girard (architect)"), a pupil of [Le Nôtre](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Le_N%C3%B4tre "André Le Nôtre"), was finally redone in the [English manner](/wiki/English_landscape_garden "English landscape garden") during the early 19th century by [Friedrich Ludwig von Sckell](/wiki/Friedrich_Ludwig_von_Sckell "Friedrich Ludwig von Sckell"), on behalf of prince-elector [Charles Theodore](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria"). Von Sckell was also the creator of the [English Garden](/wiki/Englischer_Garten "Englischer Garten") in Munich. He preserved the main elements of the Baroque garden (such as the "Grand Parterre"). The park is bisected by the long western canal along the principal axis which leads from the palace to the marble cascade (decorated with stone figures of Greek and Roman gods) in the west. The iron greenhouse north of the Grand Parterre was completed in 1807, the adjacent geranium house in 1816.

[![Overview: 1 Palace, 2 Grand parterre, 3 Crown prince garden, 4 Amalienburg, 5 Dörfchen with Brunnhaus, 6 Badenburg, 7 Monopteros, 8 Marble cascade, 9 Pagodenburg, 10 Magdalenenklause, 11 Botanical Garden](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/33/Schlosspark_Nymphenburg-Vorschau.png)](/wiki/File:Schlosspark_Nymphenburg.svg "Overview: 1 Palace, 2 Grand parterre, 3 Crown prince garden, 4 Amalienburg, 5 Dörfchen with Brunnhaus, 6 Badenburg, 7 Monopteros, 8 Marble cascade, 9 Pagodenburg, 10 Magdalenenklause, 11 Botanical Garden")

Overview: 1 Palace, 2 Grand parterre, 3 Crown prince garden, 4 Amalienburg, 5 Dörfchen with Brunnhaus, 6 Badenburg, 7 Monopteros, 8 Marble cascade, 9 Pagodenburg, 10 Magdalenenklause, 11 Botanical Garden

The garden [parterre](/wiki/Parterre "Parterre") is still a visible feature of the French garden. As part of the transformation of the entire castle grounds by Sckell it was simplified, but retained its original size. The "Grand Cascade" was built by Joseph Effner in 1717. He was referring to a concept of François Roëttiers. The water falls in the middle of a two-part water staircase, the first stage being half round to the west, the second, deeper, is formed to the east. The cascade consists of symmetry which continues through the centre channel. The right side of the cataract was covered with pink marble in 1770. Originally a supporting architecture was to be provided, which was never executed. Instead, from 1775 to 1785, sculptures were added. Many were the work of [Dominik Auliczek](/wiki/Dominik_Auliczek "Dominik Auliczek") and [Roman Anton Boos](/wiki/Roman_Anton_Boos "Roman Anton Boos"), who later added twelve decorative marble vases with mythological themes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Schlosspark_Nymphenburg_Grosse_Kaskade-2.jpg/250px-Schlosspark_Nymphenburg_Grosse_Kaskade-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Schlosspark_Nymphenburg_Grosse_Kaskade-2.jpg)

The Grand Cascade. Statue of *[Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar")* on the right, of the *[Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube")* on the left

The fountains in front of the palace and in the garden *parterre* continue to be operated by the water powered [Pumping Stations](/wiki/Pumping_Stations_at_the_Nymphenburg_Palace "Pumping Stations at the Nymphenburg Palace") built between 1803 and 1808.

The Northern Cabinet Garden is small garden that borders directly the garden side of the north wing of the main palace. It is also called *Kaisergarten*, because it is in the immediate vicinity of the rooms where Charles Albert lived during his time in Munich as [Emperor Charles VII](/wiki/Charles_VII,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VII, Holy Roman Emperor"). It has its counterpart in the Southern Cabinet Garden where [François de Cuvilliés](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Cuvilli%C3%A9s_the_Younger "François de Cuvilliés the Younger") built an octagonal bird house in 1757.

Two lakes are situated on both sides of the canal. The "Dörfchen" was created under Maximilian III Joseph as [Petit hameau](/wiki/Petit_hameau "Petit hameau"). The "Salettl" (1799), a cottage with its little garden nearby close to the former menagerie served as attraction for the children of Maximilian IV Joseph.

The garden wall (1730–1735) preserves several [Ha-ha](/wiki/Ha-ha "Ha-ha") effects. A passage close to the old [arboretum](/wiki/Arboretum "Arboretum") in the north of the Grand Parterre leads to the large *[Botanical Garden](/wiki/Botanischer_Garten_M%C3%BCnchen-Nymphenburg "Botanischer Garten München-Nymphenburg")* of Munich. Originally there was also a visual axis, the *Durchblick*, to the north-west-located [Blutenburg Castle](/wiki/Blutenburg_Castle "Blutenburg Castle").

The canals of Nymphenburg are part of the northern Munich channel system, a system of waterways that connected also to the complex of [Schleissheim Palace](/wiki/Schleissheim_Palace "Schleissheim Palace"). The endpoint of the eastern canal leading from the city to the palace forms the [Cour d'honneur](/wiki/Cour_d%27honneur "Cour d'honneur") and the centre was designed by Effner as a water parterre with a fountain, cascade and branching canals on both sides. The driveway ("Auffahrtsallee") from the city on both sides of the eastern canal is framed by a semicircle of smaller baroque buildings ("Kavalierhäuser") at the Cour d'honneur. The eastern endpoint of the canal is the *Hubertusbrunnen* (1903, a fountain building by [Adolf von Hildebrand](/wiki/Adolf_von_Hildebrand "Adolf von Hildebrand")).[[10]](#cite_note-park-10)

### Garden pavilions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Garden pavilions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg_Badenburg-3.jpg/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg_Badenburg-3.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_Nymphenburg_Badenburg-3.jpg)

Badenburg, Royal bathing house

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Nymphenburg_Amalienburg-1.jpg/250px-Nymphenburg_Amalienburg-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nymphenburg_Amalienburg-1.jpg)

[Amalienburg](/wiki/Amalienburg "Amalienburg"), Royal hunting lodge

Within the park, a number of pavilions - palaces en miniature - were built:

* The **Pagodenburg** (1716–1719) – an octagonal, two-story pavilion with [Delft](/wiki/Delft "Delft") tile decoration downstairs and [Chinoiserie](/wiki/Chinoiserie "Chinoiserie") upstairs. It was built by [Joseph Effner](/wiki/Joseph_Effner "Joseph Effner") as "maison de plaisance" and [tea house](/wiki/Tea_house "Tea house").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* The **Badenburg** (1719–1721) – a [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") pavilion also by Joseph Effner. It served for the private bathing and contains several rooms including a grand banqueting hall with a festive ornament decor by Charles Dubut and a very large tiled bath with a pool. The dressing room is decorated with various Chinese printed wallpapers. In the Monkey Cabinet the Elector performed his toilette. It was the first major building in Europe for centuries that was exclusively for the purpose of enjoying a comfortable bathroom.
* The **Magdalenenklause** – a [faux ruin](/wiki/Artificial_ruins "Artificial ruins") for retreat and meditation, erected between 1725 and 1728. The building with its prayer room is considered as an early representative of the hermitage and the ruins of architecture in Germany; it was to serve Max Emanuel as a place of contemplation - a memento mori, whose completion the elector however did not longer witness.
* The **[Amalienburg](/wiki/Amalienburg "Amalienburg")** – a [Rococo](/wiki/Rococo "Rococo") hunting lodge constructed in 1734–1739 by [François de Cuvilliés](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_de_Cuvilli%C3%A9s "François de Cuvilliés") for [Charles Albert](/wiki/Charles_VII,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles VII, Holy Roman Emperor") and his wife, [Maria Amalia of Austria](/wiki/Maria_Amalia,_Holy_Roman_Empress "Maria Amalia, Holy Roman Empress"), including a hall of mirrors (designed by Johann Baptist Zimmermann and Joachim Dietrich) and a kennel room for the hunting dogs. The building with its decoration is a definite masterpiece at the climax of European rococo.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* The **Apollotemple** – a neoclassical [monopteros](/wiki/Monopteros "Monopteros") temple by [Leo von Klenze](/wiki/Leo_von_Klenze "Leo von Klenze"), erected in 1862–1865.

The architecture of the garden pavilions was influential for other architecture in Germany. So the Wittelsbach [Falkenlust Palace](/wiki/Augustusburg_and_Falkenlust_Palaces,_Br%C3%BChl "Augustusburg and Falkenlust Palaces, Brühl") was built in the style of the Amalienburg while the Pagodenburg served as prototype for the building of the same name in [Rastatt](/wiki/Rastatt "Rastatt").

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Nymphenburg-Ecuries.jpg/250px-Nymphenburg-Ecuries.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nymphenburg-Ecuries.jpg)

Marstallmuseum Nymphenburg

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Front_Facade_of_the_Court_Stables_within_Nymphenburg_Palace_%282014%29.jpg/250px-Front_Facade_of_the_Court_Stables_within_Nymphenburg_Palace_%282014%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_Facade_of_the_Court_Stables_within_Nymphenburg_Palace_(2014).jpg)

Front Facade of the Court Stables within Nymphenburg Palace (2014)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Park_Nymphenburg.JPG/330px-Park_Nymphenburg.JPG)](/wiki/File:Park_Nymphenburg.JPG)

The grand parterre

The main building alone has more than 300,000 visitors per year.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Nymphenburg Palace has as many visitors as the [Munich Residence](/wiki/Munich_Residence "Munich Residence") and more than [Schleissheim Palace](/wiki/Schleissheim_Palace "Schleissheim Palace"), though the castles of King Ludwig II, especially [Neuschwanstein](/wiki/Neuschwanstein "Neuschwanstein"), are more frequented.

Museums:

* Schlossmuseum (Royal apartments: Central pavilion, North and South Galleries, Inner Southern Pavilion, Garden pavilions)
* [Marstallmuseum](/wiki/Marstallmuseum "Marstallmuseum") (Carriage Museum: South wing)
* Porzellanmuseum München ([Nymphenburg porcelain](/wiki/Nymphenburg_Porcelain_Manufactory "Nymphenburg Porcelain Manufactory") museum: South wing)
* [Museum of Man and Nature](/wiki/Museum_of_Man_and_Nature "Museum of Man and Nature") (North wing)
* [Erwin von Kreibig](/w/index.php?title=Erwin_von_Kreibig&action=edit&redlink=1 "Erwin von Kreibig (page does not exist)")-Museum (South Schlossrondell)

Schloss Nymphenburg is accessible by Munich public transport's tram number 17. This line passes through the city centre, including [Stachus](/wiki/Stachus "Stachus") and the main train station.

## Miscellaneous

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nymphenburg_Palace&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Miscellaneous")]

Between 1936 and 1939 open air events called "Nacht der Amazonen" ([Night of the Amazons](/wiki/Night_of_the_Amazons "Night of the Amazons")) were performed. These shows in the park comprised 2000 players with international stars, bare-breasted girls and included also members of the SS Cavalry under [Hermann Fegelein](/wiki/Hermann_Fegelein "Hermann Fegelein").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The palace and its park were some of the main filming locations of [Alain Resnais](/wiki/Alain_Resnais "Alain Resnais")'s 1961 [movie](/wiki/Film "Film") *[Last Year at Marienbad](/wiki/Last_Year_at_Marienbad "Last Year at Marienbad")*.[[16]](#cite_note-16) *[Ludwig](/wiki/Ludwig_(film) "Ludwig (film)")*, a 1972 film directed by Italian director [Luchino Visconti](/wiki/Luchino_Visconti "Luchino Visconti") about the life and death of King Ludwig II, was partly filmed in Nymphenburg.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The Dressage Facility for the [equestrian](/wiki/Equestrian_at_the_1972_Summer_Olympics "Equestrian at the 1972 Summer Olympics") events of the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics") was created in the Nymphenburg park.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The palace serves also as headquarters of the [Bavarian Administration of State-Owned Palaces, Gardens and Lakes](/wiki/Bavarian_Administration_of_State-Owned_Palaces,_Gardens_and_Lakes "Bavarian Administration of State-Owned Palaces, Gardens and Lakes").
